s0001.literal: Yin Xie: his mother was called Jiandi, a daughter of the Yousong clan, and she was Di Ku's second consort.
s0001.idiomatic: Xie of Yin had a mother named Jiandi, of the Yousong clan, who was Di Ku's junior consort.

s0002.literal: The three of them went to bathe; seeing the dark bird drop its egg, Jiandi took and swallowed it; thereby pregnant she bore Xie.
s0002.idiomatic: While Jiandi bathed with two companions, a dark bird dropped an egg; she took it, swallowed it, and conceived Xie.

s0003.literal: Xie grew up and assisted Yu in regulating the waters with merit.
s0003.idiomatic: When Xie came of age he helped Yu manage the flood and earned distinction.

s0004.literal: Emperor Shun thereupon charged Xie, saying, "The hundred surnames are not kind; the five grades lack instruction—you shall be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; the five teachings lie in breadth."
s0004.idiomatic: Emperor Shun told Xie: The people lack harmony and the five relationships lack guidance; as Minister of Education spread the five moral lessons with generous patience.

s0005.literal: He was enfeoffed at Shang and granted the surname Zi.
s0005.idiomatic: He received the fief of Shang and the clan name Zi.

s0006.literal: Xie rose in the interval of Tang, Yu, and the Great Yu; his achievements shone among the hundred surnames; by them the people were pacified.
s0006.idiomatic: He flourished from the reigns of Yao and Shun through Great Yu; his deeds won renown among the folk and kept the realm at peace.

s0007.literal: Xie passed away; his son Zhaoming established himself.
s0007.idiomatic: When Xie died, his son Zhaoming succeeded him.

s0008.literal: Zhaoming passed away; his son Xiang Tu established himself.
s0008.idiomatic: Zhaoming was succeeded by his son Xiang Tu.

s0009.literal: Xiang Tu passed away; his son Changruo established himself.
s0009.idiomatic: Xiang Tu was succeeded by his son Changruo.

s0010.literal: Changruo passed away; his son Caoyu established himself.
s0010.idiomatic: Changruo was succeeded by his son Caoyu.

s0011.literal: Caoyu passed away; his son Ming established himself.
s0011.idiomatic: Caoyu was succeeded by his son Ming.

s0012.literal: Ming passed away; his son Zhen established himself.
s0012.idiomatic: Ming was succeeded by his son Zhen.

s0013.literal: Zhen passed away; his son Wei established himself.
s0013.idiomatic: Zhen was succeeded by his son Wei.

s0014.literal: Wei passed away; his son Baoding established himself.
s0014.idiomatic: Wei was succeeded by his son Baoding.

s0015.literal: Baoding passed away; his son Baoyi established himself.
s0015.idiomatic: Baoding was succeeded by his son Baoyi.

s0016.literal: Baoyi passed away; his son Baobing established himself.
s0016.idiomatic: Baoyi was succeeded by his son Baobing.

s0017.literal: Baobing passed away; his son Zhuren established himself.
s0017.idiomatic: Baobing was succeeded by his son Zhuren.

s0018.literal: Zhuren passed away; his son Zhugui established himself.
s0018.idiomatic: Zhuren was succeeded by his son Zhugui.

s0019.literal: Zhugui passed away; his son Tianyi established himself; this is Cheng Tang.
s0019.idiomatic: Zhugui was succeeded by his son Tianyi—who is known as Cheng Tang.

s0020.literal: Cheng Tang—from Xie to Tang eight migrations.
s0020.idiomatic: From Xie to Tang the seat of the line had shifted eight times.

s0021.literal: Tang first dwelt at Bo, following former kings in residence; he made "Di's Announcement."
s0021.idiomatic: Tang first settled at Bo beside the graves of his ancestors and composed the Announcement to Di.

s0022.idiomatic: Tang led punitive expeditions against the regional lords.

s0023.literal: Lord of Ge did not sacrifice; Tang first attacked him.
s0023.idiomatic: When the lord of Ge failed to perform the sacrifices, Tang attacked him first.

s0024.literal: Tang said, "I have a saying: people look on water and see form; look on the people and know order or not."
s0024.idiomatic: Tang said: Men look into water to see their faces; look to the people to know whether rule is sound.

s0026.literal: When words can be heard, the way then advances.
s0026.idiomatic: when counsel is heard, the Way moves forward.

s0027.literal: Ruler of the state, father to the people—all who do good are at the royal court.
s0027.idiomatic: The lord who fathers his people gathers every worthy into his court.

s0029.literal: Tang said, "You who fail to reverence the command—I will greatly punish and kill you, without any sparing."
s0029.idiomatic: Tang warned: Whoever spurns my command I will strike down without mercy.

s0030.idiomatic: He composed the Tang Punitive Expeditions.

s0032.literal: Aheng desired to attach himself to Tang but had no avenue; thereupon he became a bridesmen minister for the Youxin clan, carrying cauldron and stand, using flavors to persuade Tang, bringing him to the kingly way.
s0032.idiomatic: Wishing to serve Tang but lacking an introduction, Aheng joined the Youxin bridal train as a cook, carried kettle and board, won Tang through his cooking, and drew him toward the kingly way.

s0033.literal: Another says Yi Yin was a scholar-at-home; Tang sent men to invite him with gifts; five returns then he was willing to go and follow Tang, discoursing on the Uncrowned King and the nine lords.
s0033.idiomatic: Others say Yi Yin was a recluse whom Tang invited five times before he came, then lectured on the model rulers and the Nine Masters.

s0034.literal: Tang raised and entrusted him with state government.
s0034.idiomatic: Tang handed him the administration of the realm.

s0035.literal: Yi Yin left Tang for Xia.
s0035.idiomatic: Yi Yin left Tang and went over to the Xia court.

s0036.literal: Having found fault with You-Xia, he returned to Bo.
s0036.idiomatic: Once he had taken Xia's measure, he returned to Bo.

s0037.literal: Entering by the north gate, he met Nüjiu and Nüfang; he made "Nüjiu and Nüfang."
s0037.idiomatic: He entered by the north gate, met the ladies Jiuji and Fang, and composed the pieces Nüjiu and Nüfang.

s0038.literal: Tang went out and saw the wilds spread with nets on four sides, praying saying, "From heaven below and the four quarters all enter my net."
s0038.idiomatic: Tang found a hunter who had set nets on every side and prayed that all under heaven would fall into his snares.

s0039.literal: Tang said, "Alas—that is utterly too much!"
s0039.idiomatic: Tang cried: No—that would trap every living thing!

s0040.literal: Thereupon he removed three sides, praying saying, "Want left, left."
s0040.idiomatic: He had three sides taken down and called out: If you want left, go left.

s0041.idiomatic: If you want right, go right.

s0042.literal: Not obeying command—then enter my net."
s0042.idiomatic: Only those who defy my order need enter the net.

s0043.literal: The lords hearing it said, "Tang's virtue reaches even to birds and beasts."
s0043.idiomatic: The lords said: Tang's kindness reaches even the birds and beasts.

s0045.literal: At this time Xia Jie practiced cruel government, dissolute and wild, and among the lords the Kunwu clan made rebellion.
s0045.idiomatic: In those days Jie of Xia ruled with cruelty and excess while the Kunwu lords stirred rebellion.

s0046.literal: Tang thereupon raised troops leading the lords; Yi Yin followed Tang; Tang himself grasped the battle-axe to attack Kunwu, then attacked Jie.
s0046.idiomatic: Tang then raised an army of the lords, Yi Yin at his side, and himself wielded the axe against Kunwu before marching on Jie.

s0047.literal: Tang said, "Come you multitude, come, you all listen to my words.
s0047.idiomatic: Tang addressed the host: Come, all of you, and hear my words.

s0048.literal: It is not I, the little child, who dare raise disorder; You-Xia has many crimes—I only have heard you multitudes speak—the Xia clan has guilt.
s0048.idiomatic: It is not I who dare stir revolt; Xia is heavy with crime—I have heard you yourselves say the Xia house is guilty.

s0049.literal: I fear Supreme God; I dare not fail to correct.
s0049.idiomatic: I fear Heaven and dare not withhold punishment.

s0050.literal: Now Xia has many crimes; Heaven's mandate is to strike it dead.
s0050.idiomatic: Xia's crimes are many; Heaven has ordered its destruction.

s0051.literal: Now you have a multitude; you say, 'Our lord does not pity us multitudes, casts aside our harvest toil and cuts policy.'
s0051.idiomatic: You say among yourselves: Our king does not pity us—he sets aside our field work and meddles with government.

s0052.literal: You will say, 'If there is guilt, what then shall be done?'
s0052.idiomatic: You ask: If there is fault, what can be done?

s0053.literal: The Xia king has led astray and exhausted the multitude's strength, led and seized the Xia state.
s0053.idiomatic: The king of Xia has drained your strength and plundered the Xia domain.

s0054.literal: The multitude has led slackness and discord, saying, 'When will this sun perish?'
s0054.idiomatic: The people have grown slack and bitter, crying: When will this sun set?

s0055.literal: I with you will all perish!'
s0055.idiomatic: We would rather die with you than live under him!

s0056.literal: Xia's virtue being thus, now I must go.
s0056.idiomatic: Xia's conduct is such that I must march against it.

s0057.literal: You still may join me, the One Man, to inflict Heaven's punishment—I will greatly order you.
s0057.idiomatic: Join me, the One Man, in carrying out Heaven's sentence, and I will reward you richly.

s0058.literal: You must not disbelieve—I do not break my word.
s0058.idiomatic: Do not doubt me—I keep my oath.

s0059.literal: You who do not follow the sworn words—I will put you and your kin to death, without any sparing."
s0059.idiomatic: Whoever breaks this oath I will execute, you and your families, without mercy.

s0060.literal: Thereby announcing to command the army; he made "Tang's Oath."
s0060.idiomatic: He proclaimed this to the host and composed the Oath of Tang.

s0061.literal: Thereupon Tang said, "I am very martial," and styled himself Martial King.
s0061.idiomatic: Tang declared himself strongly martial and took the title Martial King.

s0062.literal: Jie was defeated at the ruins of Yousong; Jie fled to Mingtiao; Xia's army suffered rout.
s0062.idiomatic: Jie was beaten at Yousong, fled to Mingtiao, and his army was routed.

s0063.literal: Tang thereupon attacked Sanzong, captured its precious jade; Yibo and Zhongbo made "Canon of the Treasure."
s0063.idiomatic: Tang then struck Sanzong, seized its sacred jades, and the lords Yibo and Zhongbo composed the Canon of the Treasure.

s0064.literal: Tang having conquered Xia, wished to move its earth altar, could not, and made "Xia's Earth Altar."
s0064.idiomatic: After conquering Xia, Tang wished to relocate its soil altar but could not, and composed Xia's Earth Altar.

s0066.literal: Thereupon the lords all submitted; Tang then trod the Son of Heaven position and pacified within the seas.
s0066.idiomatic: The lords all submitted; Tang took the throne and brought peace inside the seas.

s0067.literal: Tang returning reached Taijuan Tao; Zhonglei made announcements.
s0067.idiomatic: On his return Tang halted at Taijuan Tao, where Zhonglei composed the announcements.

s0068.literal: Having ended Xia's mandate, he returned to Bo and made "Tang's Announcement": "It is the third month; the king personally reached the eastern suburbs."
s0068.idiomatic: After ending Xia's mandate he returned to Bo and issued Tang's Announcement: In the third month the king came in person to the eastern suburbs.

s0069.literal: He told the lords and host of rulers: 'None shall fail to work for the people; exert yourselves in your tasks.
s0069.idiomatic: He told the assembled rulers: None of you may neglect the people; labor diligently at your duties.

s0070.literal: I will then greatly punish and kill you—do not blame me.'
s0070.idiomatic: Or I will punish you severely—do not blame me for it.

s0071.literal: Saying: 'Of old Yu and Gao Yao long toiled outside; they had merit toward the people; the people then had rest.
s0071.idiomatic: He said: Long ago Yu and Gao Yao worked far from court; they served the people, and the people found peace.

s0072.literal: East was the Yangtze, north was the Ji, west was the He, south was the Huai—the four channels were repaired; the myriad people then had dwellings.
s0072.idiomatic: East lay the Long River, north the Ji, west the Yellow River, south the Huai—the four streams were tamed and the people had homes.

s0073.literal: Prince Millet descended and sowed, farming planted the hundred grains.
s0073.idiomatic: Hou Ji brought down seed and taught farming of every grain.

s0074.literal: The three dukes all had merit toward the people; therefore afterward they had establishment.
s0074.idiomatic: The three high ministers all served the people, and their lines were founded in turn.

s0075.literal: Of old Chiyou with his great officers made disorder among the hundred surnames; the Emperor then did not grant him—there was evidence.
s0075.idiomatic: When Chiyou and his officers ravaged the people, Heaven withdrew favor—there was clear cause.

s0076.literal: The former kings' words must not go unheeded.'
s0076.idiomatic: The words of the former kings must be heeded.

s0077.literal: Saying: 'If not the Way, do not let it remain in the state—you must not resent me.'
s0077.idiomatic: He added: If you stray from the Way, you shall not remain in power—do not resent me for it.

s0078.idiomatic: Thus he charged the regional lords.

s0079.literal: Yi Yin made "All Possess One Virtue"; Jiusdan made "Bright Dwelling."
s0079.idiomatic: Yi Yin composed All Possess One Virtue; Jiusdan composed Bright Dwelling.

s0080.literal: Tang thereupon changed the first month of the year, altered robe colors, honored white, and held court in daylight.
s0080.idiomatic: Tang reformed the calendar, changed the court dress to favor white, and held audiences by day.

s0081.literal: Tang passed away; heir son Taiding had not been established then died; thereupon they established Taiding's younger brother Waibing—this is Emperor Waibing.
s0081.idiomatic: When Tang died, the heir Taiding died before he could rule, so Taiding's brother Waibing became Emperor Waibing.

s0082.literal: Emperor Waibing having assumed position three years, passed away; they established Waibing's younger brother Zhongren—this is Emperor Zhongren.
s0082.idiomatic: Waibing ruled three years and died; his brother Zhongren succeeded as Emperor Zhongren.

s0083.literal: Emperor Zhongren having assumed position four years, passed away; Yi Yin then established Taiding's son Taijia.
s0083.idiomatic: Zhongren ruled four years and died; Yi Yin then set Taiding's son Taijia on the throne.

s0084.literal: Taijia, Cheng Tang's eldest great-grandson by the proper line—this is Emperor Taijia.
s0084.idiomatic: Taijia was Cheng Tang's senior great-grandson through the main line—Emperor Taijia.

s0085.literal: Emperor Taijia first year, Yi Yin made "Instructions for Taijia," "Loose Commands," and "Gone Kings."
s0085.idiomatic: In Taijia's first year Yi Yin composed the Instructions for Taijia, the Loose Commands, and Gone Kings.

s0086.literal: Emperor Taijia having been established three years, was not enlightened, cruel and tyrannical, did not follow Tang's laws, disordered virtue; thereupon Yi Yin banished him to Tong Palace.
s0086.idiomatic: After three years Taijia proved dark, violent, and lawless; Yi Yin banished him to the Tong palace.

s0087.literal: Three years Yi Yin exercised government in proxy and held court over the lords.
s0087.idiomatic: For three years Yi Yin ruled as regent and received the lords at court.

s0088.literal: Emperor Taijia dwelling in Tong Palace three years, repented fault and blamed himself, returned to good; thereupon Yi Yin then welcomed Emperor Taijia and handed him the government.
s0088.idiomatic: Taijia spent three years in Tong, repented, and mended his ways; Yi Yin then brought him back and restored the government.

s0089.literal: Emperor Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords all returned to Yin; the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil.
s0089.idiomatic: Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords rallied to Yin again and the people were calmed.

s0090.literal: Yi Yin approved this, then made Taijia's Instructions in three pieces, praising Emperor Taijia, styling him Grand Ancestor.
s0090.idiomatic: Yi Yin praised him, composed the three Instructions of Taijia, honored him as Grand Ancestor.

s0091.literal: Grand Ancestor passed away; his son Woding established himself.
s0091.idiomatic: When the Grand Ancestor died, his son Woding succeeded.

s0092.literal: In Emperor Woding's time, Yi Yin passed away.

s0093.literal: Having buried Yi Yin at Bo, Jiusdan then instructed on Yi Yin's affairs; he made "Woding."
s0093.idiomatic: After Yi Yin was buried at Bo, Jiusdan expounded his deeds in the piece called Woding.

s0094.literal: Woding passed away; younger brother Taigeng established himself—this is Emperor Taigeng.
s0094.idiomatic: Woding died; his brother Taigeng became Emperor Taigeng.

s0095.literal: Emperor Taigeng passed away; his son Emperor Xiaojia established himself.
s0095.idiomatic: Taigeng died and his son Xiaojia ruled.

s0096.literal: Emperor Xiaojia passed away; younger brother Yongji established himself—this is Emperor Yongji.
s0096.idiomatic: Xiaojia died; his brother Yongji became Emperor Yongji.

s0097.literal: Yin's way declined; some lords did not come to court.
s0097.idiomatic: Yin's power waned and some lords stayed away from court.

s0098.literal: Emperor Yongji passed away; younger brother Taiwu established himself—this is Emperor Taiwu.
s0098.idiomatic: Yongji died; his brother Taiwu became Emperor Taiwu.

s0099.literal: Emperor Taiwu established Yizhi as minister.

s0100.literal: At Bo there was an omen: mulberry and grain grew together in the court; in one night they became as thick as a clasped hand.
s0100.idiomatic: At Bo mulberry and grain sprouted together in the courtyard and in one night grew thick as an arm.

s0101.literal: Emperor Taiwu feared and questioned Yizhi.
s0101.idiomatic: Taiwu was frightened and asked Yizhi what it meant.

s0102.literal: Yizhi said, "I have heard omens do not overcome virtue—perhaps the Emperor's government has gaps?
s0102.idiomatic: Yizhi said: I have heard that portents cannot defeat virtue—perhaps your government needs mending?

s0104.literal: Taiwu followed it, and the auspicious mulberry withered and departed.
s0104.idiomatic: Taiwu listened; the strange mulberry died away.

s0105.literal: Yizhi praised the words to Wuxian.
s0105.idiomatic: Yizhi relayed this counsel to Wuxian.

s0106.literal: Wuxian ordered the royal house with success; he made "Salted Mugwort" and "Taiwu."
s0106.idiomatic: Wuxian brought order to the royal house and composed Salted Mugwort and Taiwu.

s0107.literal: Emperor Taiwu praised Yizhi at the temple, saying he would not treat him as subject; Yizhi yielded; he made "Original Mandate."
s0107.idiomatic: Taiwu honored Yizhi in the temple as more than a mere minister; Yizhi declined, and the Original Mandate was composed.

s0108.literal: Yin revived; the lords returned to it; therefore he is called Middle Ancestor.
s0108.idiomatic: Yin flourished again and the lords rallied—hence he is called the Middle Ancestor.

s0109.literal: Middle Ancestor passed away; his son Emperor Zhongding established himself.
s0109.idiomatic: When the Middle Ancestor died, his son Zhongding succeeded.

s0110.literal: Emperor Zhongding moved the capital to Ao.
s0110.idiomatic: Zhongding moved the capital to Ao.

s0112.literal: Zuyi moved the capital to Xing.
s0112.idiomatic: Zuyi moved the seat to Xing.

s0113.literal: Emperor Zhongding passed away; younger brother Wairen established himself—this is Emperor Wairen.
s0113.idiomatic: Zhongding died; his brother Wairen became Emperor Wairen.

s0114.literal: The Zhongding annals are defective and incomplete.
s0114.idiomatic: The records of Zhongding are lost and incomplete.

s0115.literal: Emperor Wairen passed away; younger brother Hedanjia established himself—this is Emperor Hedanjia.
s0115.idiomatic: Wairen died; his brother Hedanjia became Emperor Hedanjia.

s0116.literal: In Hedanjia's time, Yin again declined.

s0117.literal: Hedanjia passed away; his son Emperor Zuyi established himself.
s0117.idiomatic: Hedanjia died and his son Zuyi ruled.

s0118.literal: Emperor Zuyi having been established, Yin again revived.
s0118.idiomatic: When Zuyi came to the throne, Yin revived once more.

s0119.idiomatic: Wuxian the shaman served in office.

s0120.literal: Zuyi passed away; his son Emperor Zuxin established himself.
s0120.idiomatic: Zuyi died and his son Zuxin ruled.

s0121.literal: Emperor Zuxin passed away; younger brother Wojia established himself—this is Emperor Wojia.
s0121.idiomatic: Zuxin died; his brother Wojia became Emperor Wojia.

s0122.literal: Emperor Wojia passed away; they established Wojia's elder brother Zuxin's son Zuding—this is Emperor Zuding.
s0122.idiomatic: Wojia died; Zuxin's son Zuding, Wojia's nephew, became Emperor Zuding.

s0123.literal: Emperor Zuding passed away; they established younger brother Wojia's son Nangeng—this is Emperor Nangeng.
s0123.idiomatic: Zuding died; Wojia's son Nangeng succeeded as Emperor Nangeng.

s0124.literal: Emperor Nangeng passed away; they established Emperor Zuding's son Yangjia—this is Emperor Yangjia.
s0124.idiomatic: Nangeng died; Zuding's son Yangjia became Emperor Yangjia.

s0125.literal: In Emperor Yangjia's time, Yin declined.

s0126.literal: From Zhongding onward they cast aside the heir-apparent and repeatedly established younger sons of the line; younger sons sometimes struggled to replace one another—nine generations in disorder; thereupon the lords none came to court.
s0126.idiomatic: From Zhongding on the rightful heir was set aside for younger brothers, who fought for the throne—nine reigns of chaos—and the lords ceased to attend court.

s0127.literal: Emperor Yangjia passed away; younger brother Pangeng established himself—this is Emperor Pangeng.
s0127.idiomatic: Yangjia died; his brother Pangeng became Emperor Pangeng.

s0128.literal: In Emperor Pangeng's time, Yin had already placed its capital north of the He; Pangeng crossed south of the He, again dwelt in Cheng Tang's old residence—thus five migrations, no fixed place.
s0128.idiomatic: Yin had already sat north of the Yellow River; Pangeng crossed south to Cheng Tang's old capital—five moves in all, never long in one place.

s0129.literal: Yin people consulted and murmured all resentful, not wishing to move.
s0129.idiomatic: The people of Yin grumbled and refused to move.

s0130.literal: Pangeng thereupon announced to the lords and great ministers, saying, "Of old the High Queen Cheng Tang with your ancestors together settled all under Heaven; laws and models can be cultivated.
s0130.idiomatic: Pangeng addressed the lords and ministers: Long ago High Tang and your forebears founded the realm together—its laws can still be honored.

s0131.literal: Cast them aside and fail to exert yourselves—how complete virtue!"
s0131.idiomatic: If you cast them aside and slacken, how will virtue be perfected?

s0132.literal: Thereupon he crossed south of the He, governed Bo, practiced Tang's government; then the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil; Yin's way again revived.
s0132.idiomatic: He crossed the river, ruled from Bo, restored Tang's policies, and the people found rest as Yin rose again.

s0133.literal: The lords came to court, because they followed Cheng Tang's virtue.
s0133.idiomatic: The lords returned to court because he followed Tang's example.

s0134.literal: Emperor Pangeng passed away; younger brother Xiaoxin established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoxin.
s0134.idiomatic: Pangeng died; his brother Xiaoxin became Emperor Xiaoxin.

s0135.literal: Emperor Xiaoxin having been established, Yin again declined.

s0136.literal: The hundred surnames missed Pangeng; they then made Pangeng in three pieces.
s0136.idiomatic: The people longed for Pangeng, and the three chapters of Pangeng were composed.

s0137.literal: Emperor Xiaoxin passed away; younger brother Xiaoyi established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoyi.
s0137.idiomatic: Xiaoxin died; his brother Xiaoyi succeeded.

s0138.literal: Emperor Xiaoyi passed away; his son Emperor Wuding established himself.
s0138.idiomatic: Xiaoyi died and his son Wuding ruled.

s0139.literal: Emperor Wuding having assumed the throne, thought to revive Yin but had not yet found his helper.
s0139.idiomatic: Wuding wished to restore Yin but had not yet found the right minister.

s0140.literal: Three years he did not speak; government decisions lay with the grand steward, to observe the state's temper.
s0140.idiomatic: For three years he held his tongue while the chief minister decided affairs, the better to read the realm.

s0141.literal: Wuding night dreamed he obtained a sage, named Yue.
s0141.idiomatic: One night Wuding dreamed of a sage named Yue.

s0142.literal: According to the dream's appearance he viewed ministers and hundred clerks—none matched.
s0142.idiomatic: He compared the vision to his officers—none fit the dream.

s0143.literal: Thereupon he sent craftsmen to seek in the wilds and obtained Yue at Fu ravine.
s0143.idiomatic: He sent artisans to search the wilds and found Yue at Fu ravine.

s0144.literal: At that time Yue was a convict laborer, building at Fu ravine.
s0144.idiomatic: Yue was then a corvée laborer working the cliffs at Fu.

s0145.literal: Presented to Wuding, Wuding said this is he.
s0145.idiomatic: When he was brought to Wuding, the king said, This is the man.

s0146.literal: Having obtained him and conversed with him, he was indeed a sage; raised to be minister—Yin state was greatly ordered.
s0146.idiomatic: They spoke and found him a sage; he was made minister and Yin was well governed.

s0147.literal: Therefore they took Fu ravine as his surname, styled Fu Yue.
s0147.idiomatic: He took his name from Fu ravine and was called Fu Yue.

s0148.literal: Emperor Wuding sacrificed to Cheng Tang; next day a flying pheasant perched on the tripod ear and crowed; Wuding feared.
s0148.idiomatic: Wuding sacrificed to Tang; the next day a pheasant lit on the cauldron's ear and cried; the king was afraid.

s0149.literal: Zuji said, "King do not worry—first cultivate government affairs."
s0149.idiomatic: Zuji said: Do not fear, my king—set your government in order first.

s0150.literal: Zuji thereupon instructed the king, saying, "Only Heaven oversees below, grounding its righteousness; it sends down years long and not long—it is not Heaven cutting short the people's lives; mid-way it cuts off their mandate.
s0150.idiomatic: Zuji lectured the king: Heaven looks down and grounds its justice in right conduct; it grants long or short life—not to cut men off in youth but to end those who stray mid-course.

s0151.literal: When the people lack virtue fitting them, refuse to hear their guilt, Heaven having attached mandate corrects their virtue—then it says what can be done.
s0151.idiomatic: When men lack fitting virtue and ignore their faults, Heaven attaches a charge to straighten their conduct—then we ask what is to be done.

s0153.literal: King's heir reverently serve the people—none is not Heaven's succession; in regular sacrifices do not be unobservant in the way of casting aside the Way.
s0153.idiomatic: The king's heir must honor the people—all live under Heaven's succession; in your regular rites never carelessly abandon the Way.

s0154.literal: Wuding cultivated government and practiced virtue; all under Heaven rejoiced; Yin's way again revived.
s0154.idiomatic: Wuding set his house in order and practiced virtue; the realm rejoiced and Yin flourished again.

s0155.literal: Emperor Wuding passed away; his son Emperor Zugeng established himself.
s0155.idiomatic: Wuding died and his son Zugeng succeeded.

s0156.literal: Zuji approved Wuding's taking the auspicious pheasant as virtue, established his temple as Gaozong, thereupon made Gaozong's Congratulatory Day and Instructions.
s0156.idiomatic: Zuji praised Wuding for turning the pheasant omen to good; his temple was titled Gaozong, and the Congratulatory Day of Gaozong and related texts were composed.

s0157.literal: Emperor Zugeng passed away; younger brother Zujia established himself—this is Emperor Jia.
s0157.idiomatic: Zugeng died; his brother Zujia became Emperor Jia.

s0158.literal: Emperor Jia dissolute and chaotic, Yin again declined.
s0158.idiomatic: Emperor Jia gave himself to vice and Yin declined again.

s0159.literal: Emperor Jia passed away; his son Emperor Linxin established himself.
s0159.idiomatic: Jia died and his son Linxin ruled.

s0160.literal: Emperor Linxin passed away; younger brother Gengding established himself—this is Emperor Gengding.
s0160.idiomatic: Linxin died; his brother Gengding succeeded.

s0161.literal: Emperor Gengding passed away; his son Emperor Wuyi established himself.
s0161.idiomatic: Gengding died and his son Wuyi ruled.

s0162.literal: Yin again left Bo, moved north of the He.
s0162.idiomatic: Yin again abandoned Bo and moved north of the Yellow River.

s0163.literal: Emperor Wuyi lacked the Way; he made a puppet and called it Heaven's Spirit.
s0163.idiomatic: Wuyi was lawless: he fashioned a straw figure and called it the god of Heaven.

s0164.literal: He gambled with it, having men act the moves.
s0164.idiomatic: He played dice against it while others moved the pieces.

s0165.literal: When Heaven's Spirit did not win, he then humiliated and shamed it.
s0165.idiomatic: When the dummy lost, he had it mocked and disgraced.

s0166.literal: He made a leather bag, filled it with blood, raised and shot it, naming it "shooting Heaven."
s0166.idiomatic: He filled a leather sack with blood, hung it up, shot it, and called the game shooting Heaven.

s0167.literal: Wuyi hunted between the He and Wei; violent thunder struck; Wuyi was killed by lightning.
s0167.idiomatic: Wuyi hunted between the Yellow and Wei rivers; a thunderbolt struck and killed him.

s0168.literal: His son Emperor Taiding established himself.

s0169.literal: Emperor Taiding passed away; his son Emperor Yi established himself.
s0169.idiomatic: Taiding died and his son Yi ruled.

s0170.literal: Emperor Yi having been established, Yin further declined.

s0171.literal: Emperor Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi; Qi's mother was lowly—he could not succeed.
s0171.idiomatic: Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi, but Qi's mother was of low rank so he could not inherit.

s0172.literal: Younger son Xin; Xin's mother was the proper queen—Xin was heir.
s0172.idiomatic: The younger son Xin had a queen mother and became heir.

s0173.literal: Emperor Yi passed away; his son Xin established himself—this is Emperor Xin; all under Heaven called him Zhou.
s0173.idiomatic: When Yi died, Xin ruled as Emperor Xin—the man the world calls Zhou.

s0174.literal: Emperor Zhou was quick of wit and tongue, keen of ear and eye;
s0174.idiomatic: Zhou was clever, eloquent, and sharp of sense;

s0175.literal: his strength surpassed others, his hands could grapple fierce beasts;
s0175.idiomatic: his strength exceeded other men and he could wrestle wild beasts with his bare hands;

s0176.literal: his knowledge enough to reject remonstrance, his words enough to gloss fault;
s0176.idiomatic: his wits let him brush off advice and his tongue varnish any fault;

s0177.literal: he boasted to ministers of his ability, raised himself above the realm in reputation, thinking all came out below himself.
s0177.idiomatic: he flaunted his talents to his ministers, looked down on the realm, and fancied none his equal.

s0178.literal: He loved wine and dissolute music, doted on women.
s0178.idiomatic: He drowned in wine and lewd music and doted on women.

s0179.literal: He loved Daji; whatever Daji said he followed.
s0179.idiomatic: He adored Daji and obeyed her every word.

s0180.literal: Thereupon he had Master Juan make new licentious sounds, northern quarter dances, dissolving music.
s0180.idiomatic: He had Master Juan compose decadent airs, dances of the northern lanes, and enervating tunes.

s0181.literal: He increased taxes to fill Lutai's coin and overflow Juliao's grain.
s0181.idiomatic: He piled taxes to stock Lutai with gold and Juliao with grain.

s0182.literal: He further gathered dogs, horses, and curiosities, filling the palace chambers.
s0182.idiomatic: He gathered dogs, horses, and rarities until the palaces burst.

s0183.literal: He further enlarged the Shaqiu park and tower, taking many wild beasts and flying birds to place within.
s0183.idiomatic: He widened the Shaqiu hunting park and tower and filled them with beasts and birds.

s0184.literal: He was negligent toward ghosts and spirits.
s0184.idiomatic: He neglected the ghosts and spirits.

s0185.literal: He greatly gathered music and games at Shaqiu, made a pool of wine, hung meat as a forest, had men and women chase each other naked among them, drinking the long night.
s0185.idiomatic: He staged revels at Shaqiu with a lake of wine, a forest of hanging meat, naked men and women sporting between them, and feasts that lasted the night through.

s0186.literal: The hundred surnames resented while lords had rebels; thereupon Zhou then doubled harsh punishments, having the paoge method.
s0186.idiomatic: The people muttered and lords rebelled; Zhou answered with crueler laws and the paoge torture.

s0187.literal: He made Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E the three dukes.
s0187.idiomatic: He named Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E as the three high dukes.

s0188.literal: The Nine Marquises had a beautiful daughter; she was sent in to Zhou.
s0188.idiomatic: The Nine Marquises offered a beautiful daughter to the court.

s0189.literal: The Nine Marquises' daughter did not love dissipation; Zhou in anger killed her and minced the Nine Marquises.
s0189.idiomatic: She disliked debauchery; Zhou killed her in fury and had the Nine Marquises ground to paste.

s0190.literal: The Marquis of E disputed it strongly, argued it swiftly; together he pickled the Marquis of E.
s0190.idiomatic: The Marquis of E remonstrated fiercely; Zhou had him pickled as well.

s0191.literal: Western Earl Chang hearing it sighed in secret.
s0191.idiomatic: When Western Earl Chang heard this he sighed in private.

s0192.literal: Chonghou Hu knew it, reported to Zhou; Zhou imprisoned Western Earl at Youli.
s0192.idiomatic: Chonghou Hu informed on him, and Zhou jailed the Western Earl at Youli.

s0193.literal: Western Earl's ministers Hongyao and others sought beauties, curiosities, and fine horses to present to Zhou; Zhou then pardoned Western Earl.
s0193.idiomatic: His men Hongyao and the rest ransomed him with beauties, treasures, and horses, and Zhou set him free.

s0194.literal: Western Earl coming out presented the lands west of the Luo, requesting removal of the paoge punishment.
s0194.idiomatic: On his release he ceded the lands west of the Luo to buy the abolition of paoge.

s0195.literal: Zhou then granted it, gave bow, arrows, axe, and halberd, allowing him to campaign, making him Western Earl.
s0195.idiomatic: Zhou agreed and gave him bow, axe, and halberd with authority to wage war as Western Earl.

s0196.literal: Yet he employed Fei Zhong to govern.
s0196.idiomatic: Yet he put Fei Zhong in charge of government.

s0197.literal: Fei Zhong was skilled at flattery, loved profit—Yin people did not cleave to him.
s0197.idiomatic: Fei Zhong flattered and grasped after gain; the Yin people did not trust him.

s0199.literal: E Lai was skilled at slander; by this the lords grew more distant.
s0199.idiomatic: E Lai excelled at malice; the lords drew farther away.

s0200.literal: Western Earl returning secretly cultivated virtue doing good; many lords rebelled against Zhou and went to join Western Earl.
s0200.idiomatic: The Western Earl went home and quietly built virtue; many lords forsook Zhou for him.

s0201.literal: Western Earl grew greater; Zhou thereby gradually lost weight of power.
s0201.idiomatic: As the Western Earl grew mighty, Zhou's grip slackened.

s0202.literal: Prince Bigan remonstrated; he would not listen.
s0202.idiomatic: Prince Bigan remonstrated; Zhou would not hear him.

s0203.literal: Shang Rong was a worthy; the hundred surnames loved him; Zhou cast him aside.
s0203.idiomatic: Shang Rong was worthy and beloved; Zhou dismissed him.

s0204.literal: When Western Earl attacked the Ji state and destroyed it, Zhou's minister Zuyi heard it and blamed Zhou, feared, ran to report to Zhou, saying, "Heaven has already ended our Yin mandate; borrowing men's great tortoise, none dares know auspicious—not former kings not aiding our posterity, only the king dissolute and cruel using self to cut off—therefore Heaven abandons us, we lack secure food, we do not think to know Heaven's nature, we do not follow lead and models.
s0204.idiomatic: When the Western Earl destroyed Ji, Zuyi blamed Zhou, fled to court, and said: Heaven has ended Yin's mandate; even the great tortoise cannot show good fortune—it is not that the former kings forsake us, but the king's cruelty has cut us off from Heaven, so we lack peace, ignore Heaven's mind, and spurn the statutes.

s0205.literal: Now our people none do not desire perdition, saying 'When will Heaven send down its might—why does the great mandate not arrive?'
s0205.idiomatic: Now none of your people but wishes this house to fall, crying: When will Heaven strike—why does the new king not come?

s0206.literal: Now what shall the king do?"
s0206.idiomatic: What will you do now, my king?

s0207.literal: Zhou said, "Was I not born with the mandate in Heaven!"
s0207.idiomatic: Zhou answered: Was I not born with Heaven's mandate?

s0208.literal: Zuyi returning said, "Zhou cannot be remonstrated with."
s0208.idiomatic: Zuyi went home saying: Zhou will not be warned.

s0209.literal: Western Earl having passed away, King Wu of Zhou campaigned east, reaching Mengjin; lords rebelling against Yin who met Zhou were eight hundred.
s0209.idiomatic: After the Western Earl died, King Wu marched east to Mengjin; eight hundred lords who had turned from Yin rallied to him.

s0210.literal: The lords all said, "Zhou can be attacked."
s0210.idiomatic: They said: Zhou is ripe for attack.

s0211.literal: Martial King said, "You do not yet know Heaven's mandate."
s0211.idiomatic: The Martial King said: You do not yet read Heaven's timing.

s0213.literal: Zhou further dissolute and chaotic without stop.
s0213.idiomatic: Zhou sank deeper into vice without end.

s0214.literal: Weizi remonstrated many times without being heard; then with the grand and junior masters he planned and departed.
s0214.idiomatic: Weizi pleaded again and again in vain, then conspired with the grand and junior masters and left.

s0215.literal: Bigan said, "Being someone's minister, one cannot but contend with death."
s0215.idiomatic: Bigan said: A minister who will not risk death is no minister.

s0217.literal: Zhou angrily said, "I have heard the sage's heart has seven openings."
s0217.idiomatic: Zhou raged: I hear a sage's heart has seven chambers.

s0218.literal: He cut open Bigan and viewed his heart.
s0218.idiomatic: He cut Bigan open to look at his heart.

s0219.literal: Jizi feared, then feigned madness as a slave; Zhou again imprisoned him.
s0219.idiomatic: Jizi feigned madness as a slave in terror; Zhou jailed him too.

s0220.literal: Yin's grand and junior masters then took their sacrificial instruments and fled to Zhou.
s0220.idiomatic: Yin's grand and junior masters fled to Zhou carrying the ritual vessels.

s0221.literal: King Wu of Zhou thereupon then led the lords to attack Zhou.
s0221.idiomatic: King Wu then led the host against Zhou.

s0222.literal: Zhou also raised troops to resist him at Muye.
s0222.idiomatic: Zhou sent an army to meet him at Muye.

s0223.literal: On the jiazi day Zhou's army was defeated.
s0223.idiomatic: On the jiazi day Zhou's host was broken.

s0224.literal: Zhou ran in, ascended Lutai, clothed himself in his jade-inlaid robes, threw himself into fire and died.
s0224.idiomatic: Zhou fled within, climbed Lutai in his jeweled robes, and burned himself to death.

s0225.literal: King Wu of Zhou thereupon beheaded Zhou, hung it on the great white banner.
s0225.idiomatic: King Wu struck off his head and raised it on the great white standard.

s0227.literal: He released Jizi from prison, sealed Bigan's tomb, marked Shang Rong's lane.
s0227.idiomatic: He freed Jizi, sealed Bigan's grave, and honored Shang Rong's lane.

s0228.literal: He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng, Lufu, to continue Yin sacrifices, ordering him to practice Pangeng's government.
s0228.idiomatic: He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng—Lufu—to keep Yin sacrifices and follow Pangeng's rule.

s0230.literal: Thereupon King Wu of Zhou became Son of Heaven.
s0230.idiomatic: King Wu then became Son of Heaven.

s0231.literal: His later generations lowered the title emperor, styling them kings.
s0231.idiomatic: Later Zhou dropped the title emperor and called its rulers kings.

s0232.literal: And he enfeoffed Yin's posterity as lords, subordinate to Zhou.
s0232.idiomatic: Yin's descendants were enfeoffed as lords under Zhou.

s0233.literal: King Wu of Zhou passed away; Wugeng with the lords of Guan and Cai made rebellion; the completed king ordered the Duke of Zhou to execute them, then established Weizi at Song to continue Yin's posterity.
s0233.idiomatic: When King Wu died, Wugeng and the lords of Guan and Cai rebelled; the young king had the Duke of Zhou crush them and set Weizi in Song to carry on Yin's line.

s0234.literal: The Grand Scribe says: I have arranged Xie's affairs by the hymns; from Cheng Tang onward drawn from the Documents and Odes.
s0234.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe remarks: I ordered Xie's story by the hymns; from Cheng Tang I drew on the Documents and the Odes.

s0235.literal: Xie was Zi surname; afterward divided enfeoffments, used states as surnames—there were Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, Moyi clans.
s0235.idiomatic: Xie's house took the name Zi; later branches used their domains as surnames, yielding houses such as Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, and Moyi.

s0236.literal: Confucius said, Yin's road carriages were finest, and the color honored was white.
s0236.idiomatic: Confucius said the Yin state carriages were the best of their day and they favored the color white.
